title: oidcProvider({ allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod: true }) — accepts 'plain' PKCE, which provides no protection against code interception

params.passDetail: allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod is false, so only the S256 code_challenge_method is accepted. S256 sends only a SHA-256 hash of the verifier in the authorization request, so intercepting the request does not reveal the verifier.
params.failDetail: allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod: true lets clients use code_challenge_method='plain', where the code_challenge equals the raw code_verifier. An attacker who can read the authorization request (the exact threat PKCE exists to defeat) sees the plain verifier and can redeem an intercepted authorization code — plain PKCE gives zero protection. RFC 7636 recommends S256; OAuth 2.1 disallows plain. Fix: remove this option or set allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod: false.
params.absentCallDetail: No oidcProvider() call in this scope; rule N/A here.
params.absentArgDetail: oidcProvider() is called but allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod is not set; verify the resolved default and prefer S256-only.

provenance.note: In authorize.ts the allowed methods are `options.allowPlainCodeChallengeMethod ? ['s256','plain'] : ['s256']`, and a missing method even defaults to 'plain' when this flag is on. RFC 7636 §4.2 says S256 MUST be used if the client can; 'plain' is a downgrade. AI agents enable this to make a non-conforming client work, silently neutering PKCE.
